---
title: "Module 0: Welcome"
format: 
  revealjs:
    scrollable: true
    smaller: true
    toc: false
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Welcome to SISMID Workshop: R Programming for Infectious Disease Modeling!

</br>

**Alex Edwards (Any)**

Instructor, Department of Biostatistics and Bioinformatics,
Emory University

Email: Alex.edwards\@emory.edu

</br>

**Zane Billings (he/him)**

Data Scientist, State Farm

Email: wz.billings\@gmail.com

## Introductions

-   Name?
-   Current position / institution?
-   Most interesting pathogen/condition/exposure to you?

## Course website

-   All of the materials for this course can be found online here:
    [https://wzbillings.github.io/SISMID-2026/](https://wzbillings.github.io/SISMID-2026/).
-   This contains the schedule, course resources, and online versions of
    all of our slide decks.
-   Please feel free to download these resources and share them -- all
    of the course content is under the [Creative Commons BY-NC 4.0
    license](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/).

## Overall Workshop Objectives

By the end of this workshop, you should be able to

1. Write code that uses advanced R programming tools like control flow and custom functions.
2. Use advanced R programming tools to answer interesting epidemiology questions.
3. Be (more) prepared to use R in other SISMID modules

## Workshop Overview

Multiple modules that will each:

-   Start with learning objectives
-   End with summary slides
-   Include mini-exercises or practice problems

Themes that will show up throughout the workshop:

-   Reproducibility
-   Good coding techniques
-   Thinking algorithmically

The 2026 core schedule is set, and we also keep a few optional or take-home topics available for people who want extra practice. What are **you** most interested in learning?

## Course structure

- Day 1 core: Quarto, statistics in R, iteration, functions, data science walkthrough 1, and Exercise 1.
- Day 2 core: functional programming, useful packages, S3/formulas, data science walkthrough 2, Exercise 1 continuation, and Exercise 2.
- Day 3 core: ODEs in R, disease mapping/GIS, and time for questions.
- Our website lists additional free resources for learning R in a stats and epi context, and also has some more advanced materials you can use to practice what you've learned if you want.

## Using AI in This Course

AI tools are welcome in this course. Use them as learning partners for coding, debugging, explanation, planning, and checking your work.

- **You are responsible for everything you submit**: code, analysis, figures, and interpretation.
- Use AI to deepen your learning, **not to skip the thinking**. You should be able to explain and revise your work.
- Be transparent. Acknowledge meaningful AI help, including code, text, analysis choices, or editing.
- Cite or link any non-original code, text, data, or ideas, whether from AI, websites, examples, papers, or other people.
- Do not upload private, sensitive, or unpublished data unless you know it is permitted.

## Installing R

Hopefully everyone has pre-installed R and RStudio (and Quarto if needed). Let's take a minute to make sure everyone is set up and then jump in!
